devanagari: जमदग्निवधः परशुरामद्वारा क्षत्रियाणां संहारः विश्वामित्र वंशवर्णनं च । श्रीशुक उवाच । पित्रोपशिक्षितो रामः तथेति कुरुनन्दन । संवत्सरं ती...
english_devnagari: jamadagnivadhaḥ paraśurāmadvārā kṣatriyāṇāṃ saṃhāraḥ viśvāmitra vaṃśavarṇanaṃ caśrīśuka uvācapitropaśikṣito rāmaḥ tatheti kurunandanasaṃvatsaraṃ tīrthayātrāṃ caritvā'śramamāvrajat
translation:
Śrī Śuka continued:
Rāma who was thus admonished by his father, consented (with words) “Be it so”. After spending one year in visiting holy places, he returned to his hermitage, Oh princes of the Kuru race!

devanagari: कदाचित् रेणुका याता गंगायां पद्ममालिनम् । गन्धर्वराजं क्रीडन्तं अप्सरोभिरपश्यत ॥ २ ॥
english_devnagari: kadācit reṇukā yātā gaṃgāyāṃ padmamālinamgandharvarājaṃ krīḍantaṃ apsarobhirapaśyata
translation: On one occasion, when Reṇukā went to the Gaṅgā (for bringing water), she happened to see Citraratha, the king of Gandharvas, adorned with a wreath of lotuses, sporting with celestial nymphs (in the water).

devanagari: विलोकयन्ती क्रीडन्तं उदकार्थं नदीं गता । होमवेलां न सस्मार किञ्चित् चित्ररथस्पृहा ॥ ३ ॥
english_devnagari: vilokayantī krīḍantaṃ udakārthaṃ nadīṃ gatāhomavelāṃ na sasmāra kiñcit citrarathaspṛhā
translation: Reṇukā who came to the river to fetch water, conceived just a passing longing for Citraratha, and forgot the time fixed for the Homa (sacrificial worship) while gazing at the sporting king.

devanagari: कालात्ययं तं विलोक्य मुनेः शापविशंकिता । आगत्य कलशं तस्थौ पुरोधाय कृताञ्जलिः ॥ ४ ॥
english_devnagari: kālātyayaṃ taṃ vilokya muneḥ śāpaviśaṃkitāāgatya kalaśaṃ tasthau purodhāya kṛtāñjaliḥ
translation: Realizing the delay caused thereby, and being afraid of the sage’s (Jamadagni’s) curse, she hastened back, kept the pitcher of water before him, and remained standing with folded palms.

devanagari: व्यभिचारं मुनिर्ज्ञात्वा पत्‍न्याः प्रकुपितोऽब्रवीत् । घ्नतैनां पुत्रकाः पापां इत्युक्तास्ते न चक्रिरे ॥ ५ ॥
english_devnagari: vyabhicāraṃ munirjñātvā pat‍nyāḥ prakupito'bravītghnataināṃ putrakāḥ pāpāṃ ityuktāste na cakrire
translation: Gathering the faithlessness of his wife, the sage got enraged and commanded, “Oh sons! Kill this sinful woman”. Despite this order, none obeyed.

devanagari: रामः सञ्चोदितः पित्रा भ्रातॄन् मात्रा सहावधीत् । प्रभावज्ञो मुनेः सम्यक् समाधेस्तपसश्च सः ॥ ६ ॥
english_devnagari: rāmaḥ sañcoditaḥ pitrā bhrātṝn mātrā sahāvadhītprabhāvajño muneḥ samyak samādhestapasaśca saḥ
translation: Rāma who was fully aware of the powers of the sage through his spiritual meditation and penance, promptly despatched his brothers along with his mother, when directed to do so by his father.

devanagari: वरेण च्छन्दयामास प्रीतः सत्यवतीसुतः । वव्रे हतानां रामोऽपि जीवितं चास्मृतिं वधे ॥ ७ ॥
english_devnagari: vareṇa cchandayāmāsa prītaḥ satyavatīsutaḥvavre hatānāṃ rāmo'pi jīvitaṃ cāsmṛtiṃ vadhe
translation: Jamadagni, the son of Satyavatī, was pleased with him (Paraśurāma) and asked him to seek a boon. Rāma requested the boon that all those who had been killed, should be restored to life, and should not remember who slew them.

devanagari: उत्तस्थुस्ते कुशलिनो निद्रापाय इवाञ्जसा । पितुर्विद्वान् तपोवीर्यं रामश्चक्रे सुहृद्वधम् ॥ ८ ॥
english_devnagari: uttasthuste kuśalino nidrāpāya ivāñjasāpiturvidvān tapovīryaṃ rāmaścakre suhṛdvadham
translation: Immediately they got up hale and hearty as if from a sound sleep. Rāma had killed his kith and kin (mother and brothers), as he had known the spiritual power of his father.

devanagari: येऽर्जुनस्य सुता राजन् स्मरन्तः स्वपितुर्वधम् । रामवीर्यपराभूता लेभिरे शर्म न क्वचित् ॥ ९ ॥
english_devnagari: ye'rjunasya sutā rājan smarantaḥ svapiturvadhamrāmavīryaparābhūtā lebhire śarma na kvacit
translation: Oh king! The sons of Arjuna who, being vanquished by the valour of Rāma, had fled, continued to brood over the killing of their father (by Rāma) and found no mental peace anywhere.

devanagari: एकदाश्रमतो रामे सभ्रातरि वनं गते । वैरं सिसाधयिषवो लब्धच्छिद्रा उपागमन् ॥ १० ॥
english_devnagari: ekadāśramato rāme sabhrātari vanaṃ gatevairaṃ sisādhayiṣavo labdhacchidrā upāgaman
translation: On one occasion, when Paraśurāma along with his brothers had left for the forest, they (the sons of Arjuna), desirous of avenging (for the killing of their father by Rāma), found a (favourable) opportunity, and entered the hermitage.

devanagari: दृष्ट्वाग्न्यगार आसीनं आवेशितधियं मुनिम् । भगवति उत्तमश्लोके जघ्नुस्ते पापनिश्चयाः ॥ ११ ॥
english_devnagari: dṛṣṭvāgnyagāra āsīnaṃ āveśitadhiyaṃ munimbhagavati uttamaśloke jaghnuste pāpaniścayāḥ
translation: Determined to murder Jamadagni, they saw the sage sitting with his mind concentrated upon the glorious Lord of excellent renown, in the sacrificial hall, and killed him.

devanagari: याच्यमानाः कृपणया राममात्रातिदारुणाः । प्रसह्य शिर उत्कृत्य निन्युस्ते क्षत्रबन्धवः ॥ १२ ॥
english_devnagari: yācyamānāḥ kṛpaṇayā rāmamātrātidāruṇāḥprasahya śira utkṛtya ninyuste kṣatrabandhavaḥ
translation: Though piteously entreated by the helpless mother of Paraśurāma, those hard-hearted dregs of the Kṣatriya community, forcibly severed his head and carried it away.

devanagari: रेणुका दुःखशोकार्ता निघ्नन्त्यात्मानमात्मना । राम रामेति तातेति विचुक्रोशोच्चकैः सती ॥ १३ ॥
english_devnagari: reṇukā duḥkhaśokārtā nighnantyātmānamātmanārāma rāmeti tāteti vicukrośoccakaiḥ satī
translation: Overwhelmed with grief and deep- anguish, and beating herself with her own hands, the chaste lady Reṇukā screamed loudly, “Oh Rāma! Rāma! come (quickly) Oh child”.

devanagari: तदुपश्रुत्य दूरस्था हा रामेत्यार्तवत्स्वनम् । त्वरयाऽश्रममासाद्य ददृशुः पितरं हतम् ॥ १४ ॥
english_devnagari: tadupaśrutya dūrasthā hā rāmetyārtavatsvanamtvarayā'śramamāsādya dadṛśuḥ pitaraṃ hatam
translation: Hearing from afar, a distressful call “Oh Rāma”, Rāma hastened to the hermitage to find his father killed.

devanagari: ते दुःखरोषामर्षार्ति शोकवेगविमोहिताः । हा तात साधो धर्मिष्ठ त्यक्त्वास्मान् स्वर्गतो भवान् ॥ १५ ॥
english_devnagari: te duḥkharoṣāmarṣārti śokavegavimohitāḥhā tāta sādho dharmiṣṭha tyaktvāsmān svargato bhavān
translation: Overwhelmed and confounded with sorrow, rage, impetuosity, affliction and intense grief, Rāma bewailed “Oh. saintly father! Oh righteous-most sire! Leaving us behind here, you proceeded to heaven.”

devanagari: विलप्यैवं पितुर्देहं निधाय भ्रातृषु स्वयम् । प्रगृह्य परशुं रामः क्षत्रान्ताय मनो दधे ॥ १६ ॥
english_devnagari: vilapyaivaṃ piturdehaṃ nidhāya bhrātṛṣu svayampragṛhya paraśuṃ rāmaḥ kṣatrāntāya mano dadhe
translation: After lamenting thus, Paraśurāma entrusted the body of his father to his brothers. Taking his axe with him, he determined to exterminate the Kṣatriya race entirely.

devanagari: गत्वा माहिष्मतीं रामो ब्रह्मघ्नविहतश्रियम् । तेषां स शीर्षभी राजन् मध्ये चक्रे महागिरिम् ॥ १७ ॥
english_devnagari: gatvā māhiṣmatīṃ rāmo brahmaghnavihataśriyamteṣāṃ sa śīrṣabhī rājan madhye cakre mahāgirim
translation: Sallying forth to the city of Māhiṣmatī, the splendour of which was already lost due to the murder of a Brāhmaṇa (Jamadagni), he built up (as it were) a huge mountain of the heads of the sons of Sahasrārjuna in the heart of the city.

devanagari: तद् रक्तेन नदीं घोरां अब्रह्मण्यभयावहाम् । हेतुं कृत्वा पितृवधं क्षत्रेऽमंगलकारिणि ॥ १८ ॥
english_devnagari: tad raktena nadīṃ ghorāṃ abrahmaṇyabhayāvahāmhetuṃ kṛtvā pitṛvadhaṃ kṣatre'maṃgalakāriṇi
translation: With the blood of those (Haihayas), he created a terrible river which aroused fear in the heart of the enemies of Brāhmaṇas. When the Kṣatriyas had taken to wicked (inauspicious) ways, he made the murder of his father as a cause of action and completely annihilated the Kṣatriyas thrice seven times[1] from the earth, and caused (the creation of) pools of blood[2] in Samantapañcaka (Kurukṣetra) Oh King.
purport:
Footnote [1]:
Bhāvāratha Dīpikā informs us that according to tradition, Reṇukā beat her breast twenty one times, and hence Rāma destroyed Kṣatriyas as many times.

Footnote [2]:
Bhāgavata Candrikā states that the v.l. hradān nava is wrong, as the place is called Samanta (Syamanta)-pañcaka and not Samanta (Syamanta)-navaka and the Mahābhārata Ādi. records Samanta (Syamanta)-pañcaka as the name 2.4-11.

devanagari: त्रिःसप्तकृत्वः पृथिवीं कृत्वा निःक्षत्रियां प्रभुः । समन्तपञ्चके चक्रे शोणितोदान् ह्रदान् नृप ॥ १९ ॥
english_devnagari: triḥsaptakṛtvaḥ pṛthivīṃ kṛtvā niḥkṣatriyāṃ prabhuḥsamantapañcake cakre śoṇitodān hradān nṛpa
translation: With the blood of those (Haihayas), he created a terrible river which aroused fear in the heart of the enemies of Brāhmaṇas. When the Kṣatriyas had taken to wicked (inauspicious) ways, he made the murder of his father as a cause of action and completely annihilated the Kṣatriyas thrice seven times[1] from the earth, and caused (the creation of) pools of blood[2] in Samantapañcaka (Kurukṣetra) Oh King.
purport:
Footnote [1]:
Bhāvāratha Dīpikā informs us that according to tradition, Reṇukā beat her breast twenty one times, and hence Rāma destroyed Kṣatriyas as many times.

Footnote [2]:
Bhāgavata Candrikā states that the v.l. hradān nava is wrong, as the place is called Samanta (Syamanta)-pañcaka and not Samanta (Syamanta)-navaka and the Mahābhārata Ādi. records Samanta (Syamanta)-pañcaka as the name 2.4-11.

devanagari: पितुः कायेन सन्धाय शिर आदाय बर्हिषि । सर्वदेवमयं देवं आत्मानं अयजन्मखैः ॥ २० ॥
english_devnagari: pituḥ kāyena sandhāya śira ādāya barhiṣisarvadevamayaṃ devaṃ ātmānaṃ ayajanmakhaiḥ
translation: Recovering the head of his father (taken away by the sons of Arjuna), he joined it to the trunk of his father’s body. Keeping it on the Kuśa grass, he worshipped by performing sacrifices, the Deity, his own Soul, who represents all gods in his person.

devanagari: ददौ प्राचीं दिशं होत्रे ब्रह्मणे दक्षिणां दिशम् । अध्वर्यवे प्रतीचीं वै उद्‍गात्रे उत्तरां दिशम् ॥ २१ ॥
english_devnagari: dadau prācīṃ diśaṃ hotre brahmaṇe dakṣiṇāṃ diśamadhvaryave pratīcīṃ vai ud‍gātre uttarāṃ diśam
translation: (It is verily reported that) as dakṣiṇā (gift for performance of sacrifices), he gave the eastern quarter (the eastern part of the earth) to the Hotṛ, the southern quarter to the Brahmā, the western direction to the Adhvaryu and the northern direction to the Udgātṛ.

devanagari: अन्येभ्योऽवान्तरदिशः कश्यपाय च मध्यतः । आर्यावर्तं उपद्रष्ट्रे सदस्येभ्यस्ततः परम् ॥ २२ ॥
english_devnagari: anyebhyo'vāntaradiśaḥ kaśyapāya ca madhyataḥāryāvartaṃ upadraṣṭre sadasyebhyastataḥ param
translation: To others he donated (lands in) the intermediate quarters, and the central portion of the earth to sage Kaśyapa, Aryāvarta (region between the Himalayas and the Vindhya mountain) to the sacrificial supervisors (upadraṣṭas) and the land lying beyond that to Sadasyas (judges or superintendents of sacrifices.).

devanagari: ततश्चावभृथस्नान विधूताशेषकिल्बिषः । सरस्वत्यां महानद्यां रेजे व्यभ्र इवांशुमान् ॥ २३ ॥
english_devnagari: tataścāvabhṛthasnāna vidhūtāśeṣakilbiṣaḥsarasvatyāṃ mahānadyāṃ reje vyabhra ivāṃśumān
translation: Having washed off all his sins in the conclusive ablution at the end of the sacrifice in the river Sarasvatī, the embodiment of the Veda, he shone resplendent like the sun with clouds dispelled.

devanagari: स्वदेहं जमदग्निस्तु लब्ध्वा संज्ञानलक्षणम् । ऋषीणां मण्डले सोऽभूत् सप्तमो रामपूजितः ॥ २४ ॥
english_devnagari: svadehaṃ jamadagnistu labdhvā saṃjñānalakṣaṇamṛṣīṇāṃ maṇḍale so'bhūt saptamo rāmapūjitaḥ
translation: Having regained his (spiritual) body characterised by pure consciousness, Jamadagni became the seventh star[3] in the sphere of sages in the sky (Ursa Major), and was worshipped by Rāma.
purport:
Footnote [3]:
VC. gives the list of seven sages presiding over the seven stars in the constellation Ursa Major as follows: Kāśyapa, Atri, Vasiṣṭha, Viśvāmitra, Gautama, Bharadvāja and Jamadagni.

devanagari: जामदग्न्योऽपि भगवान् रामः कमललोचनः । आगामिन्यन्तरे राजन् वर्तयिष्यति वै बृहत् ॥ २५ ॥
english_devnagari: jāmadagnyo'pi bhagavān rāmaḥ kamalalocanaḥāgāminyantare rājan vartayiṣyati vai bṛhat
translation: (It is said that) verily this son of Jamadagni, the glorious Rāma of lotus-like eyes, will be the propagator of the Vedas—one of the seven sages, the authorities over the Vedas—in the coming Manvantara.

devanagari: आस्तेऽद्यापि महेन्द्राद्रौ न्यस्तदण्डः प्रशान्तधीः । उपगीयमानचरितः सिद्धगन्धर्वचारणैः ॥ २६ ॥
english_devnagari: āste'dyāpi mahendrādrau nyastadaṇḍaḥ praśāntadhīḥupagīyamānacaritaḥ siddhagandharvacāraṇaiḥ
translation: Even today he resides on the Mahendra mountain. He has laid down the rod of punishment, and is now perfectly serene in mind. His deeds are being sung by Siddhas, Gandharvas and Cāraṇas.

devanagari: एवं भृगुषु विश्वात्मा भगवान् हरिरीश्वरः । अवतीर्य परं भारं भुवोऽहन् बहुशो नृपान् ॥ २७ ॥
english_devnagari: evaṃ bhṛguṣu viśvātmā bhagavān harirīśvaraḥavatīrya paraṃ bhāraṃ bhuvo'han bahuśo nṛpān
translation: In this way, the glorious Lord Hari, the Ruler of the world, the very Soul of (all beings in) the Universe incarnated Himself in the Bhṛgu clan, and lightened the burden of the earth by killing several times the Kṣatriyas (or lords of men).

devanagari: गाधेरभूत् महातेजाः समिद्ध इव पावकः । तपसा क्षात्रमुत्सृज्य यो लेभे ब्रह्मवर्चसम् ॥ २८ ॥
english_devnagari: gādherabhūt mahātejāḥ samiddha iva pāvakaḥtapasā kṣātramutsṛjya yo lebhe brahmavarcasam
translation: Gādhi (the King of Kānyakubja) begot a son (Viśvāmitra) whose superb majestic personality was brilliant like a blazing fire. By virtue of his asceticism and austere penance, he transcended his Kṣatriya status and attained the glory of Brahmanism.

devanagari: विश्वामित्रस्य चैवासन् पुत्रा एकशतं नृप । मध्यमस्तु मधुच्छन्दा मधुच्छन्दस एव ते ॥ २९ ॥
english_devnagari: viśvāmitrasya caivāsan putrā ekaśataṃ nṛpamadhyamastu madhucchandā madhucchandasa eva te
translation: Viśvāmitra had a hundred sons. Out of them Madhucchandas was the middle, but all of them are known by the same (group) name Madhucchandas, Oh King!

devanagari: पुत्रं कृत्वा शुनःशेपं देवरातं च भार्गवम् । आजीगर्तं सुतानाह ज्येष्ठ एष प्रकल्प्यताम् ॥ ३० ॥
english_devnagari: putraṃ kṛtvā śunaḥśepaṃ devarātaṃ ca bhārgavamājīgartaṃ sutānāha jyeṣṭha eṣa prakalpyatām
translation: Having adopted as his son Śunaḥśepa, the son of Ajīgarta of the Bhṛgu clan, who was given to him by gods and hence named as Devarāta, he told his sons, “Let this (Śunaḥśepa) be regarded by you as your eldest (brother).[4]
purport:
Footnote [4]:
As Bhāvāratha Dīpikā points out, this explains why Devarāta is one of the Pravara’s of the Kauśika Gotra, even though as a rule a Pravara belongs to the same Gotra.

devanagari: यो वै हरिश्चन्द्रमखे विक्रीतः पुरुषः पशुः । स्तुत्वा देवान् प्रजेशादीन् मुमुचे पाशबन्धनात् ॥ ३१ ॥
english_devnagari: yo vai hariścandramakhe vikrītaḥ puruṣaḥ paśuḥstutvā devān prajeśādīn mumuce pāśabandhanāt
translation: It was verily the same person who was sold as a human ‘sacrificial beast’ (victim) for the sacrifice of Hariścandra[5] and who through his prayer[6] to gods and Lords of creation (like Varuṇa) was released from the bondage of cords (that tied him to the sacrificial post).
purport:
Footnote [5]:
Vide Supra 9.7. 20-21.

Footnote [6]:
e.g. Sūktas like Ṛg Veda Saṃhitā. 1.25.

devanagari: यो रातो देवयजने देवैर्गाधिषु तापसः । देवरात इति ख्यातः शुनःशेपस्तु भार्गवः ॥ ३२ ॥
english_devnagari: yo rāto devayajane devairgādhiṣu tāpasaḥdevarāta iti khyātaḥ śunaḥśepastu bhārgavaḥ
translation: He who was protected and given by gods in the sacrifice offered to Varuṇa, came to be known as Devarāta among the Gādhi family and that ascetic was known as Śunḥśepa in the family of Bhṛgus.

devanagari: ये मधुच्छन्दसो ज्येष्ठाः कुशलं मेनिरे न तत् । अशपत् तान् मुनिः क्रुद्धो म्लेच्छा भवत दुर्जनाः ॥ ३३ ॥
english_devnagari: ye madhucchandaso jyeṣṭhāḥ kuśalaṃ menire na tataśapat tān muniḥ kruddho mlecchā bhavata durjanāḥ
translation: The brothers who were elder than Madhucchandas did not approve of it (the order of Viśvāmitra to recognize Devarāta as their eldest brother). The enraged sage cursed them, “Ye wicked fellows! Be ye all mlecchas (outcast barbarians)”.

devanagari: स होवाच मधुच्छन्दाः सार्धं पञ्चाशता ततः । यन्नो भवान् संजानीते तस्मिन् तिष्ठामहे वयम् ॥ ३४ ॥
english_devnagari: sa hovāca madhucchandāḥ sārdhaṃ pañcāśatā tataḥyanno bhavān saṃjānīte tasmin tiṣṭhāmahe vayam
translation: It is reported that, thereupon Madhucchandas along with his fifty brothers submitted, “we shall accept the status and abide by whatever you command us.”

devanagari: ज्येष्ठं मन्त्रदृशं चक्रुः त्वां अन्वञ्चो वयं स्म हि । विश्वामित्रः सुतानाह वीरवन्तो भविष्यथ । ये मानं मेऽनुगृह्णन्तो वीरवन्तमकर्त माम्...
english_devnagari: jyeṣṭhaṃ mantradṛśaṃ cakruḥ tvāṃ anvañco vayaṃ sma hiviśvāmitraḥ sutānāha vīravanto bhaviṣyathaye mānaṃ me'nugṛhṇanto vīravantamakarta mām
translation: To the seer of Vedic mantras (Śunaḥśepa) they told him, “We are all your younger brothers.” And thus they recognized him as their eldest brother. Viśvāmitra (being highly pleased) said to his sons, “As you have maintained my respect as your father, and have made me worthy as a father with heroic sons, may you all be blessed with progeny”.

devanagari: एष वः कुशिका वीरो देवरातस्तमन्वित । अन्ये चाष्टकहारीत जयक्रतुमदादयः ॥ ३६ ॥
english_devnagari: eṣa vaḥ kuśikā vīro devarātastamanvitaanye cāṣṭakahārīta jayakratumadādayaḥ
translation: “Oh descendents of Kauśika family, this Devarāta is one of you (as a scion of your family); obey his orders.” Viśvāmitra had also other sons viz. Aṣṭaka, Hārīta, Jaya, Kratumat and others.

devanagari: एवं कौशिकगोत्रं तु विश्वामित्रैः पृथग्विधम् । प्रवरान्तरमापन्नं तद्धि चैवं प्रकल्पितम् ॥ ३७ ॥
english_devnagari: evaṃ kauśikagotraṃ tu viśvāmitraiḥ pṛthagvidhampravarāntaramāpannaṃ taddhi caivaṃ prakalpitam
translation:
In this way the Kauśika family came to be divided into different branches through the sons of Viśvāmitra, and it had another different Pravara due to the adoption of Devarāta.[7]
[back to top]
purport:
Footnote [7]:
It appears that there had been different Viśvāmitras in widely distant ages. e.g. Viśvāmitra, the father of Śakuntalā, Viśvāmitra the teacher of Daśaratha’s son Rāma, Viśvāmitra the contemporary of Hariścandra, Jamadagni and Śunaḥśepa, and Viśvāmitra and the Dāśarājña. The Purāṇas, however, have rolled these distinct personalities into one, and have caused confusion and chaos among the students of Purāṇas. It is true that there was some rivalry between the Viśvāmitras and Vasiṣṭhas in the Ṛg Veda Saṃhitā, but the Purāṇas have added a lot of new material unknown to the Vedas and the Brāhmaṇas.
